clusters.title: Former governor-general Peter Hollingworth dies at the age of 91
clusters.neutral_headline: Former governor-general Peter Hollingworth dies at the age of 91
bullet_summary: Reported by 9News, The Age, ABC News Australia

items.title: Former governor-general Peter Hollingworth dies at the age of 91
items.standfirst: Former governor-general and retired Anglican bishop Peter Hollingworth has died at the age of 91.
items.content: Former governor-general and Anglican bishop Dr Peter Hollingworth has died at the age of 91.Hollingsworth was first ordained an Anglican priest in 1960 and became decon-in-charge of St Mary's in North Melbourne.Over the course of decades, he rose up through ranks of the church and was elected the Anglican Archbishop of Brisbane in 1989.READ MORE: Hundreds of Australians infected with rare, deadly disease He joined independent Anglican welfare organisation, the Brotherhood of St Laurence, as a chaplain and director for youth and children's work in 1964, and progressed to a director of social policy and research. He was appointed as executive director to the organisation in 1980 and completed a master's degree in social work.His work culminated in an Australian of the Year award in 1991.READ MORE: 'Doomsday' glacier is on the verge of collapse and scientists are worried He was later appointed as Australia's 23rd governor-general in 2001 under former prime minister John Howard.However Hollingworth resigned from the position in 2003 amid controversy of his handling of child abuse in the church.The Anglican Church of Southern Queensland said in a statement it sent its condolences to Hollingworth's family and acknowledged with "deep regret the past failings of the church"."In 2003 a board of inquiry into the handling of complaints of sexual abuse in the Anglican Diocese of Brisbane found that, as Archbishop, Hollingworth failed to act on knowledge of sexual abuse and allowed two clergy to remain in the church, despite knowing they had sexually assaulted children," the church said.READ MORE: Dodgy booze that killed teens found in local liquor stores "The board of inquiry also found that Bishop Hollingworth had not handled a complaint from Beth Heinrich regarding the conduct of Donald Shearman 'fairly, reasonably or appropriately'."Anglican Church of Southern Queensland acknowledges with deep regret the past failings of the church."Anglican Church of Southern Queensland apologises unreservedly to those who have suffered abuse, distress, isolation, and harm caused by the church's failure to respond with integrity and care when it was needed most."Hollingworth's wife Ann died in 2021.The couple had three daughters together.NEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here

items.title: Peter Hollingworth, former governor-general and Anglican archbishop, dies at 91
items.standfirst: Australia’s 23rd governor-general resigned less than two years into the role, after pressure over his handling of child sex abuse complaints while archbishop of Brisbane.
items.content: Australia’s 23rd governor-general resigned less than two years into the role, after pressure over his handling of child sex abuse complaints while archbishop of Brisbane.

items.title: Former governor-general Peter Hollingworth dies aged 91
items.standfirst: Peter Hollingworth was the first and only cleric to serve as governor-general, before his resignation in 2003 amid his handling of historical church abuse scandals.
items.content: Peter Hollingworth was the first and only cleric to serve as governor-general, before his resignation in 2003 amid his handling of historical church abuse scandals.

clusters.title: School students rescued from camp as deluge floods two states
clusters.neutral_headline: School students rescued from camp as deluge floods two states

items.title: School students rescued from camp as deluge floods two states
items.standfirst: A group of school students were rescued from a flooded campground after a sudden deluge across south-east Queensland and northern NSW.
items.content: A group of school students were rescued from a flooded campground after a sudden deluge across south-east Queensland and northern NSW.

items.title: School students rescued from camp as deluge floods two states
items.standfirst: T he school said in a statement that all their students were safe and offered praise for emergency responders.
items.content: A group of school students were rescued from a flooded campground after a sudden deluge across south-east Queensland and northern NSW.Hundreds of millimetres of rain have fallen in both regions in the past two days, with multiple riverine flooding warnings in place on either side of the border.Emergency services have carried out multiple rescues.LIVE UPDATES: Eleven Aussies on board aid ships intercepted by Israeli navyAmong them were a group of about 50 Year 12 students and teachers from North Lakes State College, who were on a camping trip at Mount Barney, near the Queensland-NSW border."During the return from activities, rapidly changing weather conditions impacted access to the camp accommodation site," the school said in a new statement posted to social media this morning."Emergency services, including the State Emergency Service (SES), Queensland Police Service (QPS), Queensland Fire Department (QFD) and Queensland Ambulance Service (QAS), assisted staff and students to safely return to the SES base.READ MORE: Confronting scenes inside Sydney home where woman, two kids killed"Students and staff remained safe throughout the incident and were transported back to the College overnight, where students were reunited with their families."This morning, the NSW SES also said it had responded to 197 incidents and carried out three rescues involving people and vehicles trapped in floodwaters at Numinbah, Brewongle, and Goongerry."We've already seen several flood rescues that could have ended very badly," SES Assistant Commissioner Dean Storey said.WORLD: Five killed in hail of bullets at US mosque"Thanks to the quick response from NSW SES and our emergency service partners, everyone was brought to safety."He said the SES continued to work with local communities to prepare for further flooding and rain, and urged people to avoid the roads."The safest thing people can do right now is stay where they are and avoid travelling until floodwaters go down," he said. READ MORE: Major city's property market plummets to lockdown lowsFalls of up to 240mm were recorded in the cross-border region in the past couple of days. "A near-stationary low pressure trough being fed with moisture-laden winds caused persistent and heavy showers over southeast Qld and northeast NSW on Sunday and Monday," Weatherzone said."Drier weather will return from Tuesday afternoon and night as the system moves off the country's east coast."NEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here

clusters.title: Will the war in Iran make Australia use less plastic?
clusters.neutral_headline: Will the war in Iran make Australia use less plastic

items.title: Will the war in Iran make Australia use less plastic?
items.standfirst: Could the war in Iran change the way we recycle back home? Australia mostly relies on importing plastic from overseas. As well as petrol and diesel, the Strait of Hormuz is a key delivery route for a crucial backbone of plastic production. But, that's now cut off. It's led to a growing conversation about how Australia might transition to fully recycling plastic packaging onshore.
items.content: Could the war in Iran change the way we recycle back home? Australia mostly relies on importing plastic from overseas. As well as petrol and diesel, the Strait of Hormuz is a key delivery route for a crucial backbone of plastic production. But, that's now cut off. It's led to a growing conversation about how Australia might transition to fully recycling plastic packaging onshore.
